title: Best USB Hubs for Desktops 2026
excerpt: Running out of USB ports kills productivity. We tested the best USB hubs of 2026 — from compact travel hubs to powered desktop expansions — to find your ideal solution.
author: CompareElite Staff
intro: Modern computers ship with fewer USB-A ports than ever, replaced by USB-C and Thunderbolt connections. Whether you're dealing with a slim laptop that has just two ports or a desktop that somehow can't accommodate all your peripherals, a USB hub is the practical solution. But not all hubs are equal: underpowered hubs cause devices to drop out, slow hubs bottleneck transfer speeds, and cheap hubs can damage connected devices. We tested 15 USB hubs across different use cases to find the best options for home offices, travel, and power users in 2026.

buying_guide.title: USB-C vs USB-A Hubs: Which Do You Need?
buying_guide.body: USB-C hubs connect to your computer via a USB-C port and typically provide a mix of USB-A, USB-C, HDMI, SD card, and Ethernet ports. They're essential for modern laptops that have replaced most USB-A ports with USB-C. USB-A hubs connect via a traditional USB-A port and add more USB-A ports — useful for desktops with limited USB-A slots. Many hubs today are hybrids, connecting via USB-C but offering USB-A downstream ports for compatibility with older peripherals. If you have a modern laptop, a USB-C hub is almost certainly what you need. If you have a desktop with USB-A ports but need more, a powered USB-A hub is the simpler, more affordable choice.

buying_guide.title: Powered vs Bus-Powered Hubs
buying_guide.body: Bus-powered hubs draw power from the computer's USB port — convenient (no extra adapter) but limited. USB-A ports provide 0.5W to 0.9W; USB-C provides up to 15W. Bus-powered hubs are fine for low-power devices like keyboards, mice, and flash drives but will struggle with hard drives, charging phones at full speed, or running multiple power-hungry devices simultaneously. Powered hubs include an external AC adapter, providing 60–100W or more of stable power to connected devices. For desktop setups with multiple peripherals, a powered hub is strongly recommended. For travel use with a laptop (keyboard, mouse, USB stick), a compact bus-powered hub is simpler and lighter.

buying_guide.title: Data Transfer Speeds: USB 2.0 vs 3.0 vs 3.2
buying_guide.body: USB 2.0 transfers at 480 Mbps — adequate for keyboards, mice, webcams, and audio interfaces, but painfully slow for file transfers from external drives. USB 3.0 (also labeled USB 3.1 Gen 1 or USB 3.2 Gen 1) transfers at 5 Gbps — fine for most external SSDs and HDDs. USB 3.2 Gen 2 runs at 10 Gbps, and USB 3.2 Gen 2×2 hits 20 Gbps — needed for high-speed external NVMe drives. For maximum transfer speeds, both the hub AND your computer's USB controller need to support the same standard. If your laptop has USB 3.0 ports, a USB 3.2 hub won't magically give you 10 Gbps — you're limited to the slowest link in the chain. Thunderbolt 3/4 hubs unlock 40 Gbps for maximum performance.

buying_guide.title: Power Delivery Pass-Through
buying_guide.body: For laptop users, power delivery pass-through (PD) is a critical feature. PD pass-through lets the hub supply power to your laptop via the same USB-C connection it uses for data — meaning one cable from your dock to your laptop handles everything. Look for 85W+ PD pass-through for Intel laptops; Apple Silicon MacBooks need 96W+ for fast charging. Some hubs 'reserve' 15–30W from the rated PD output for their own operation — a hub rated for 100W PD may only deliver 70W to your laptop. Check actual laptop charging wattage in reviews, not just the spec sheet number. Thunderbolt 4 docks typically offer the most reliable high-wattage PD pass-through.

buying_guide.title: Build Quality and Heat Management
buying_guide.body: Cheap USB hubs run hot, which degrades performance and shortens lifespan. Quality hubs use aluminum enclosures that dissipate heat passively — you'll notice them getting warm but not burning to the touch. Plastic-body hubs trap heat, leading to performance throttling or device disconnects during intensive use. For hubs that stay connected permanently on a desk, aluminum construction is worth the premium. For occasional travel use, lightweight plastic is more practical. Cable quality matters too: hubs with short, stiff cables limit placement flexibility. Look for hubs with braided cables of at least 20cm length for comfortable desk positioning.

buying_guide.title: Specific Port Types Worth Paying For
buying_guide.body: Beyond standard USB-A and USB-C ports, consider which specialty ports genuinely add value to your workflow. Ethernet port: essential for stable video calls and fast downloads on a laptop without a built-in Ethernet jack. HDMI/DisplayPort: lets you connect an external monitor without a separate adapter. SD/microSD slots: valuable for photographers and videographers who regularly transfer media cards. 3.5mm audio jack: useful for laptops that removed their headphone port. USB-C with PD: lets you charge USB-C devices at fast-charge speeds. Choose a hub that covers your actual workflow needs rather than one with the most ports on paper — excess ports add size and cost without benefit.

verdict: The Anker 555 USB-C Hub is our top pick for a versatile powered desktop hub — 8 ports including 100W PD, dual HDMI, and high-speed USB 3.0/3.1 fit most home office needs. For travel, the Anker 332 is the slim, reliable workhorse. Power users with Thunderbolt laptops should consider the CalDigit TS4 for full 98W laptop charging and comprehensive connectivity.

products.name: Anker 555 USB-C Hub (8-in-1)
pros: 8 ports: 100W USB-C PD, 4K HDMI, USB 3.0 x2, USB 2.0, SD, microSD, Ethernet
100W USB-C Power Delivery pass-through for full laptop charging
Compact aluminum body runs cool even under load
No external power adapter needed — bus-powered via USB-C
Broad compatibility with MacBook, Windows laptops, and iPads
cons: No second display output — single HDMI only
Bus-powered limits peak output to connected devices

products.name: CalDigit TS4 Thunderbolt 4 Dock
pros: 18 ports total via single Thunderbolt 4 connection
98W laptop charging with Thunderbolt 4
2.5G Ethernet for multi-gigabit networking
Dual Thunderbolt 4 downstream for daisy-chaining
Supports dual 4K or single 8K display output
cons: Requires Thunderbolt 4 laptop — no benefit on USB-C only machines
Large desktop footprint for a hub

products.name: Anker 332 USB-C Hub (5-in-1)
pros: Ultra-compact slim profile fits any laptop bag pocket
4K HDMI + USB-C PD + 2x USB-A + USB-C data
85W USB-C PD pass-through for laptop charging
6-month warranty + Anker reliability track record
No external power needed
cons: Only 5 ports — not ideal for fixed desk setups
No Ethernet or SD card slots

products.name: SABRENT 60W 7-Port USB-C Hub
pros: 60W external power adapter for stable charging across all ports
7 ports including USB-C PD, USB 3.2, and USB 2.0
Individual power switches per port for device management
LED indicators show port activity
Excellent for charging multiple devices simultaneously
cons: Requires external power adapter — less portable
Larger form factor than bus-powered competitors

products.name: Hiearcool USB-C Hub 11-in-1
pros: 11 ports covering every common connection type
Dual HDMI output for multi-monitor setup
SD and TF (microSD) card slots
USB-A and USB-C downstream ports
100W USB-C PD pass-through
cons: HDMI outputs share bandwidth — both limited to 1080p simultaneously
Runs hotter than competitors under full load
Build quality below premium hubs at this price

products.name: Plugable 7-in-1 USB-C Hub with 2.5G Ethernet
pros: 2.5 Gbps Ethernet — 2.5x faster than standard gigabit
4K60 HDMI for crisp external display output
100W USB-C PD pass-through
USB 3.0 x2 and USB 2.0 x1 downstream
Excellent driver support across macOS, Windows, ChromeOS
cons: Only 7 ports — fewer than similarly-priced competitors
2.5G Ethernet requires a 2.5G router/switch to benefit

faq.q: How many devices can I safely connect to a USB hub?
faq.a: Theoretically, USB allows up to 127 devices per controller. Practically, the limit is power. Bus-powered hubs provide roughly 0.5–0.9W per port from the host's USB allocation — enough for low-power devices like keyboards, mice, and flash drives. For power-hungry devices (external hard drives need 0.9W+, phones need 5–10W to charge), use a powered hub with its own AC adapter. As a general rule: if you're connecting more than 4 devices or any device that charges via USB, use a powered hub.

faq.q: Will a USB hub slow down my internet or USB transfer speed?
faq.a: USB hubs share bandwidth between connected devices. If you plug in a USB 3.0 SSD and a USB 3.0 flash drive simultaneously, they share the hub's total bandwidth (5 Gbps for USB 3.0). Each device operating alone gets the full speed; both running simultaneously share it. For internet: a USB Ethernet adapter in a hub gets the hub's full bandwidth — no slowdown unless other devices are simultaneously transferring large amounts of data. For most everyday use, a quality USB 3.0 hub won't create noticeable bottlenecks.

faq.q: Do I need a Thunderbolt hub or is USB-C enough?
faq.a: Thunderbolt 4 hubs (like the CalDigit TS4) offer 40 Gbps bandwidth, 98W laptop charging, and the ability to daisy-chain Thunderbolt devices. They're worth the premium if you work with multiple 4K monitors, NVMe external drives at full speed, or need maximum laptop power delivery. For most users — a monitor, keyboard, mouse, webcam, and phone charging — a quality USB-C hub at $40–$60 provides identical practical performance at a fraction of the Thunderbolt dock price. Only invest in Thunderbolt if you'll use its specific capabilities.

faq.q: Why do devices disconnect from my USB hub?
faq.a: Device disconnections from USB hubs are almost always caused by insufficient power. The connected devices are collectively drawing more current than the hub can supply, causing it to reset. Solutions: (1) use a powered hub instead of a bus-powered one, (2) reduce the number of power-hungry devices, (3) disable USB selective suspend in Windows power settings, (4) replace a cheap hub with a quality model from Anker, Plugable, or CalDigit. Heat can also cause disconnections — if your hub feels very hot, improve its ventilation or replace it.

faq.q: Can a USB hub charge my laptop?
faq.a: Yes, if the hub supports USB Power Delivery (USB-C PD) and your laptop accepts USB-C charging. Look for hubs with PD pass-through rated at 85W+ for most laptops (MacBook Air needs 30W minimum, MacBook Pro needs 96W for fast charging, most Windows ultrabooks need 65–90W). The hub routes power from a USB-C charger through to your laptop via the same connection it uses for data. This single-cable connection — one USB-C cable handling power, data, and display — is the main advantage of a USB-C hub over older USB-A hub designs.
